edges.description: Floor-of-mouth edema and tongue elevation precipitate stridor and dyspnea, requiring airway protection.
edges.source: Tongue Elevation and Airway Narrowing from Floor Edema

nodes.description: Empiric IV antibiotics targeting polymicrobial oral flora.

evidence.reference_title: Diagnosis and management of Ludwig's angina: An evidence-based review.
evidence.snippet: Broad spectrum antibiotics and surgical source control are keys in treating the infection.

evidence.reference_title: The empirical use of aminoglycosides in Ludwig's angina based on bacterial analysis of 63 cases.
evidence.snippet: Ninety-two percent (n=57/62) of the streptococci cultured were sensitive to penicillin. The addition of aminoglycosides to the study sample would not have made a statistically significant difference (p=0.1556).

nodes.description: The streptococci and oral anaerobes that drive Ludwig's angina build their peptidoglycan cell wall by penicillin-binding protein (PBP) transpeptidase cross-linking. This essential, bacteria-specific reaction is the target of the penicillin/beta-lactam component of empiric broad-spectrum therapy; culture series show the predominant streptococci remain penicillin-sensitive, so acylation of the PBP active site halts cross-linking and is bactericidal.

evidence.snippet: although glycan chain polymerization occurs in the absence of transpeptidation (for example, in the presence of penicillin), efficient transpeptidation requires ongoing GTase reactions

nodes.description: Submandibular and sublingual edema elevate the tongue and floor of mouth, narrowing the oropharyngeal airway and precipitating stridor or dyspnea.
nodes.id: Tongue Elevation and Airway Narrowing from Floor Edema

evidence.snippet: elevation of the floor of the mouth that caused an obstruction of the airway and resulted in an inspiratory dyspnea and a stridor.
